# Provenance Guard

A backend that any creative-sharing platform can call to estimate whether submitted text was
**written by a human or generated by AI**, score that estimate honestly, show readers a
plain-language **transparency label**, and give creators a way to **appeal**. Built for AI201
Project 4.

Design priority throughout: **a false positive (calling a human's work AI) is the worst error
on a writing platform.** The thresholds, the dynamic uncertainty band, and the label wording
are all shaped by that asymmetry.

## Quick start

```bash
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
python app.py                 # serves on http://localhost:5001 (set port=5001 in app.py)
```

Verify the detector on the canonical test inputs: `python test_signals.py`.

## Architecture

See [`planning.md`](./planning.md) for the full architecture diagram and spec. In short:
`POST /submit` → three detection signals → `combine()` (ai_likelihood + confidence) →
attribution → transparency label → audit log → response. `POST /appeal` flips status to
`under_review` and logs the appeal beside the original decision.

| Endpoint | Accepts | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| `POST /submit` | `{text, creator_id}` | `{content_id, attribution, confidence, ai_likelihood, signals, label, status}` |
| `POST /appeal` | `{content_id, creator_reasoning}` | `{content_id, status, message, original_decision}` |
| `GET /log` | `?limit=N` | `{entries:[...]}` |
| `GET /analytics` | — | detection patterns + appeal rate *(stretch)* |
| `GET /health` | — | liveness + whether the LLM signal is enabled |

**Example `/submit` response** (the clearly-AI input — note the structured JSON, the label text,
and all three individual signal scores returned alongside the combined result):

```json
{
  "content_id": "ba6fcd08-f8fc-4426-b840-8b43b09b023f",
  "attribution": "likely_ai",
  "confidence": 0.458,
  "ai_likelihood": 0.784,
  "signals": { "llm_score": 0.8, "stylometric_score": 0.615, "lexical_score": 1.0, "llm_available": true },
  "label": {
    "variant": "high_ai",
    "text": "🤖 Likely AI-generated. Our automated check estimates roughly a 78% likelihood that this text was produced with substantial help from an AI writing tool. This is an automated estimate, not a certainty — if you wrote this yourself, you can appeal and a human will review it."
  },
  "status": "classified"
}
```

## Detection signals (multi-signal pipeline)

Three genuinely distinct signals — semantic, structural, lexical — each emitting P(AI) in
`[0,1]`. Why three independent views beat one: the LLM reads *meaning*, stylometry reads
*structure*, and the lexical check reads *word choice*, so a weakness in one is usually covered
by another.

1. **LLM semantic (Groq `llama-3.3-70b-versatile`)** — holistic "does this read machine-made?"
   judgment. *Reliable but* over-trusts polished human prose and needs an API call (degrades
   gracefully if the key is missing).
2. **Stylometric structure (pure Python)** — burstiness (sentence-length variation),
   type-token ratio, informality density, mean sentence length. AI text is uniform; human text
   is bursty. *Blind spot:* mistakes formal/uniform human writing for AI.
3. **Lexical AI-tells (pure Python)** — density of LLM cliché phrases ("it is important to
   note," "furthermore," "delve," "stakeholders," "paradigm shift"). *Blind spot:* easily
   edited away; high precision, low recall.

**Why these:** the LLM and stylometry are explicitly recommended as a strong semantic+structural
pair; adding the lexical signal is cheap, independent, and catches the most common AI register
directly. **Ensemble weighting:** `ai_likelihood = 0.60·LLM + 0.25·stylo + 0.15·lexical`
(re-normalized if the LLM is unavailable) — the LLM is the best single judge, the others correct
and corroborate it.

## Confidence scoring with uncertainty

The system keeps two numbers distinct on purpose:

- **`ai_likelihood`** — P(AI); sets the *direction* (AI vs human).
- **`confidence`** — how sure we are of the *verdict*, **not** P(AI). It is
  `|ai_likelihood − 0.5|·2`, then **reduced in proportion to how much the signals disagree.**

So `confidence = 0.6` means the signals agree and lean clearly one way, but not overwhelmingly.
The key mechanism is that **signal disagreement widens a dynamic "uncertain" band** around 0.5
(half-width `0.12 + 0.45·disagreement`): when the signals conflict, the verdict becomes
`uncertain` instead of a forced call. Asserting **AI** also requires an extra `+0.04` margin
over the human side — the false-positive guard. Text under 25 words is forced to `uncertain`.

This is why a 0.51 and a 0.95 produce different labels: 0.95 clears the band comfortably and
yields a directional label; 0.51 sits inside the (possibly widened) band and yields the
*Uncertain* label. The AI/human labels additionally **display the estimated likelihood**
(`ai_likelihood` for AI, `1 − ai_likelihood` for human) because "~78% likely AI" is more
meaningful to a non-technical reader than a raw verdict-confidence number.

**How meaningfulness was tested.** Four deliberately different inputs were run end to end with
the real Groq signal (`python test_signals.py`). The scores spread across the range and reach
all three labels, and — critically — the formal-human paragraph is **not** called AI:

| Input | LLM | stylo | lexical | ai_likelihood | confidence | attribution | label shown |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Clearly AI (paradigm/stakeholders) | 0.80 | 0.61 | 1.00 | 0.78 | **0.46** | likely_ai | 🤖 Likely AI (~78%) |
| Clearly human (ramen review) | 0.20 | 0.27 | 0.00 | 0.19 | **0.54** | likely_human | ✍️ Likely human (~81%) |
| Formal human (econ paragraph) | 0.80 | 0.73 | 0.00 | 0.66 | **0.20** | uncertain | ❓ Uncertain |
| Lightly edited AI (remote work) | 0.20 | 0.54 | 0.00 | 0.26 | **0.35** | uncertain | ❓ Uncertain |

*Two contrasting cases for the record:* the **clearly-human** review scores `confidence 0.54 →
Likely human-written`, while the **formal-human** econ paragraph scores `confidence 0.20 →
Uncertain` — a noticeably lower confidence — even though the LLM signal rated it 0.80 (AI-leaning)
on its own. The lexical signal found zero AI tells, the resulting disagreement widened the
uncertain band, and the system declined to accuse a human. That gap between a confident call and
a withheld one is the scoring doing its job, and it directly reflects the false-positive priority.

## Transparency label — three variants (verbatim)

`{pct}` is the estimated likelihood as a percentage. The AI variant is hedged and always offers
the appeal path.

| Variant | Exact text |
|---|---|
| **High-confidence AI** | `🤖 Likely AI-generated. Our automated check estimates roughly a {pct}% likelihood that this text was produced with substantial help from an AI writing tool. This is an automated estimate, not a certainty — if you wrote this yourself, you can appeal and a human will review it.` |
| **High-confidence human** | `✍️ Likely human-written. Our automated check estimates roughly a {pct}% likelihood that a person wrote this text; no strong AI-generation signals stood out. Automated estimates can still be wrong, and any creator may appeal a result.` |
| **Uncertain** | `❓ Uncertain. Our automated check could not reach a confident conclusion about whether this text was written by a person or an AI tool — the signals were mixed or too weak to call. We are showing this honestly rather than guessing; no attribution is being asserted.` |

Example rendered (AI case): *"🤖 Likely AI-generated. Our automated check estimates roughly a
78% likelihood that this text was produced with substantial help from an AI writing tool..."*

## Appeals workflow

Any creator can appeal with the `content_id` from their `/submit` response and a free-text
`creator_reasoning`. `POST /appeal` validates the id (404 if unknown), sets `content.status` →
`under_review`, and writes an `appeal` audit entry carrying the reasoning **alongside the
original attribution and confidence**, so a human reviewer sees the original verdict, the signal
scores, and the creator's argument together via `GET /log`. No automated re-classification.

```bash
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:5001/appeal -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"content_id":"ba6fcd08-f8fc-4426-b840-8b43b09b023f","creator_reasoning":"I wrote this myself; English is my second language so my style is more formal."}'
```

Real response:

```json
{
  "content_id": "ba6fcd08-f8fc-4426-b840-8b43b09b023f",
  "status": "under_review",
  "message": "Appeal received. This content is now queued for human review.",
  "original_decision": { "attribution": "likely_ai", "confidence": 0.458 }
}
```

## Rate limiting

Applied to `POST /submit` via Flask-Limiter (in-memory), keyed on client IP:

```python
@limiter.limit("10 per minute;100 per day")
```

**Reasoning for these specific numbers.** A real creator submits their own work occasionally —
a few pieces in a sitting at most, even when revising and resubmitting. **10/minute** is far
above honest human use, so legitimate creators never hit it, but it stops a script from
hammering the (paid, LLM-backed) endpoint. **100/day** caps sustained abuse from a single
source while still accommodating a power user or a small editorial team behind one IP. The two
limits cover both **burst** abuse (the per-minute cap) and **slow-drip** abuse (the per-day
cap). `/appeal` is separately limited to `20/hour` — appeals should be rare per creator.
*Production note:* limits would be keyed on authenticated `creator_id` with shared (Redis)
storage rather than per-IP in-memory.

**Evidence** (12 rapid requests; first 10 pass, rest are throttled):

```
$ for i in $(seq 1 12); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" -X POST \
    http://localhost:5001/submit -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"text":"...","creator_id":"ratelimit-test"}'; done
200
200
200
200
200
200
200
200
200
200
429
429
```

## Audit log

Every decision and appeal is written to a structured SQLite table and exposed at `GET /log`
(most-recent-first). Each entry records timestamp, content id, creator, event type,
attribution, confidence, **all three individual signal scores**, the combined `ai_likelihood`,
label variant, status, and any appeal reasoning. Real `GET /log` excerpt (the appeal beside its
original `likely_ai` decision for the same `content_id`, plus one of each other variant):

```json
{
  "entries": [
    {
      "id": 11, "content_id": "ba6fcd08-...", "creator_id": "ai-writer",
      "timestamp": "2026-06-29T05:21:21.935Z", "event_type": "appeal",
      "attribution": "likely_ai", "confidence": 0.458, "ai_likelihood": 0.784,
      "llm_score": null, "stylometric_score": null, "lexical_score": null,
      "label_variant": null, "status": "under_review",
      "appeal_reasoning": "I wrote this myself; English is my second language so my style is more formal."
    },
    {
      "id": 10, "content_id": "d07160e9-...", "creator_id": "econ-prof",
      "timestamp": "2026-06-29T05:20:20.546Z", "event_type": "submission",
      "attribution": "uncertain", "confidence": 0.13, "ai_likelihood": 0.603,
      "llm_score": 0.7, "stylometric_score": 0.73, "lexical_score": 0.0,
      "label_variant": "uncertain", "status": "classified", "appeal_reasoning": null
    },
    {
      "id": 9, "content_id": "fcc36180-...", "creator_id": "human-1",
      "timestamp": "2026-06-29T05:20:20.304Z", "event_type": "submission",
      "attribution": "likely_human", "confidence": 0.518, "ai_likelihood": 0.195,
      "llm_score": 0.2, "stylometric_score": 0.301, "lexical_score": 0.0,
      "label_variant": "high_human", "status": "classified", "appeal_reasoning": null
    },
    {
      "id": 8, "content_id": "ba6fcd08-...", "creator_id": "ai-writer",
      "timestamp": "2026-06-29T05:20:19.873Z", "event_type": "submission",
      "attribution": "likely_ai", "confidence": 0.458, "ai_likelihood": 0.784,
      "llm_score": 0.8, "stylometric_score": 0.615, "lexical_score": 1.0,
      "label_variant": "high_ai", "status": "classified", "appeal_reasoning": null
    }
  ]
}
```

The `appeal` entry (id 11) carries the original verdict beside the creator's reasoning; the
matching `submission` entry (id 8, same `content_id`) holds the individual signal scores.

## Stretch features implemented

- **Ensemble detection (3+ signals):** three weighted independent signals (semantic +
  structural + lexical), documented weighting `0.60 / 0.25 / 0.15` with re-normalization when
  the LLM is unavailable. See *Detection signals*.
- **Analytics view (`GET /analytics`):** returns total submissions, counts by attribution,
  appeals filed, **appeal rate**, items under review, and average confidence — a lightweight
  detection-pattern dashboard over the audit log.

Real `GET /analytics` output after a test session:

```json
{
  "total_submissions": 20,
  "by_attribution": { "likely_ai": 4, "likely_human": 3, "uncertain": 13 },
  "appeals_filed": 1,
  "appeal_rate": 0.05,
  "content_under_review": 1,
  "avg_confidence": 0.306
}
```

## Known limitations

The clearest failure mode is **formal, structurally uniform human writing** — academic prose,
technical documentation, or writing by **non-native English speakers** who favor a more formal
register. This trips the stylometric signal directly: low burstiness, few contractions, and
long even sentences are exactly its AI cues. The system *contains* this (the LLM weight, the
disagreement-widened uncertain band, and the extra AI-side margin push such text to *Uncertain*
rather than a false *AI* call) but it cannot fully *solve* it — genuinely formal human writing
will sometimes land in *Uncertain* when a human reader would confidently say "person wrote
this." A second known weakness is **very short text** (poems, micro-fiction): with too few
sentences, stylometry is undefined and the LLM is unreliable, so the system honestly returns
*Uncertain* rather than guessing.

A third, subtler one observed in live testing: the **LLM signal is not perfectly deterministic
even at temperature 0** — the same econ paragraph scored `llm_score 0.8` on one run and `0.7`
on another (both still resolved to *Uncertain*). This is a property of hosted inference, not a
bug, and it is part of why the system never trusts a single signal: the structural and lexical
signals plus the disagreement band absorb that wobble instead of letting one noisy score decide.

## Spec reflection

- **One way the spec helped:** writing the three label variants *and* their thresholds in
  `planning.md` before any code forced the central design decision early — that `confidence`
  should mean "certainty of the verdict," not raw P(AI). Implementing scoring against fixed
  label text made the dynamic uncertainty band fall out naturally instead of being bolted on.
- **One way the implementation diverged:** the spec's example audit entry implies
  `confidence ≈ llm_score` (P(AI)). I diverged: `confidence` is verdict-certainty reduced by
  signal disagreement, and P(AI) is reported separately as `ai_likelihood`. Reason — a single
  P(AI) number can't express "the signals disagree, so trust this less," which is exactly what
  a transparency system owes its users. I also added a min-length guard and graceful LLM
  degradation that the spec didn't mention.

## AI usage

1. **Pipeline + scoring (M3–M4):** I gave the AI my §1 Detection signals and §2 Uncertainty
   spec plus the architecture diagram and asked it to generate the Flask skeleton, the signal
   functions, and the `combine()` scoring logic. I **overrode** its first scoring approach,
   which mapped raw P(AI) directly to confidence; I replaced it with verdict-certainty +
   disagreement-widened band after testing showed the formal-human paragraph would have been
   falsely labeled AI. I also tuned the stylometric weights against the four canonical inputs.
2. **Labels + appeals (M5):** I asked the AI to generate `make_label()` and the `/appeal`
   endpoint from my §3/§4 spec. I **revised** the label wording it produced to hedge the AI
   variant and always surface the appeal path (false-positive protection), and adjusted the
   appeal logging so the original decision is carried onto the appeal entry for reviewers.

## Files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `app.py` | Flask API: `/submit`, `/appeal`, `/log`, `/analytics`, `/health`, rate limiting |
| `detection.py` | Three signals, ensemble combine, classification, label mapping |
| `db.py` | SQLite audit log + content status |
| `test_signals.py` | Milestone-4 verification harness  |
| `planning.md` | Spec: signals, uncertainty, labels, appeals, edge cases, architecture, AI plan |
| `requirements.txt`, `.env.example`, `.gitignore` | Setup |
